segments.title: Wolford Chapel: a Canadian heritage site in Devon
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke introduces Wolford Chapel, a Devon chapel linked to John Graves Simcoe and Upper Canada that is now owned by Ontario.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke makes an unusual exception to film inside Wolford Chapel because the building itself illustrates why historical places deserve attention. He explains that historians publicize places, including on social media, to encourage people to visit them. He describes the chapel as a small piece of Canada in the United Kingdom, built in 1802 on the Simcoe family estate. The chapel commemorates John Graves Simcoe's service as the first lieutenant governor of Upper Canada. Ontario Heritage took it over in 1982, making the chapel a tangible connection between Canadian and British history. Dr. Clarke also presents it as a memorial to what Canadians and Britons have achieved together. The moment shows how a modest building can carry institutional, family, and transatlantic historical associations that are easy to miss without visiting it.

evidence.note: The transcript explains historians' public outreach, identifies the chapel, links it to John Graves Simcoe and Upper Canada, and describes its Ontario ownership and Canadian-British associations.

segments.title: Why visits help preserve historical places
segments.summary: An empty visitor book at accessible Wolford Chapel anchors Dr. Clarke's case that attention and visits help preserve both historic places and their stories.
segments.body: The empty visitor book makes the preservation problem concrete: Dr. Clarke was the only person recorded as visiting Wolford Chapel that day. The site is small, but it is clearly marked, accessible, and normally open, so its lack of visitors cannot be blamed simply on being unreachable. He argues that history not visited or paid attention to can be forgotten and lost. When that happens, the stories and lessons attached to a place disappear from public awareness as well. Historians therefore highlight sites and events, including through social media, to turn attention into visits. Those visits help keep both the place and its history visible, giving future visitors a reason to return and discover it for themselves.

evidence.note: The transcript uses the empty visitor book, the chapel's accessibility, and the risk of forgotten stories to explain why historians encourage visits to historical sites.
